attributes.stem: A 56-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a five-day history of pain and swelling around his right eye. He has a medical history of type 2 diabetes and hypertension. He takes metformin and amlodipine regularly. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His visual acuity is 6/6 bilaterally and he has normal extraocular movements. There is erythema and swelling overlying the inferomedial aspect of his right orbit (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The image shows erythema and swelling overlying the lacrimal sac in the inferomedial orbit, which is consistent with acute dacryocystitis. Dacryocystitis occurs secondary to blockage of the nasolacrimal duct, causing stasis of tears in the lacrimal sac and predisposing it to infection. The patient's history of diabetes is relevant, as it increases susceptibility to infections, including dacryocystitis.

Orbital cellulitis typically presents with more diffuse periocular swelling, potential optic nerve compression, and restriction of extraocular movements. In this case, the localised nature of the swelling and normal extraocular movements make orbital cellulitis less likely.

A hordeolum (stye) is an acute folliculitis of an eyelash follicle, usually presenting as a small, painful bump on the eyelid margin. The location and extent of swelling in this case are not consistent with a hordeolum.

A chalazion presents as a solitary, non-tender nodule in the tarsal plate due to blocked meibomian glands. It typically develops over weeks to months and is not associated with the acute onset and inflammation seen in this case.

Episcleritis is characterised by redness overlying the sclera without the localised swelling seen in this image. It also tends to cause less pain than dacryocystitis.

Treatment of acute dacryocystitis involves systemic antibiotic therapy to address the infection, followed by surgical interventions aimed at relieving the nasolacrimal duct obstruction. The choice of antibiotic should consider coverage for common pathogens such as Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pneumoniae. In patients with diabetes, close monitoring of blood glucose levels is important during treatment.
learningPoints: Dacryocystitis occurs secondary to lacrimal duct obstruction and presents with erythema and swelling over the lacrimal sac in the inferomedial orbit.
Acute dacryocystitis is treated with systemic antibiotics followed by surgical intervention to relieve nasolacrimal duct obstruction.
Diabetes is a risk factor for dacryocystitis due to increased susceptibility to infections.
references: Bowling, B. (2015). Kanski’s clinical ophthalmology (8th ed.). W B Saunders.
